speaker: interviewer
text: --how you got to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Plaszow. Well, I went with another group that I supposed to-- because I was sick, OK? So then I went with another group and I knew where I belong. You know, they told me you are in number this and this, and so on. And I was with nice people around me, very nice. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- how did you got to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you. We had-- how did we get-- well, we had horse and buggy or something like that what they transported us. Some people walked. I don't remember if I walked. Maybe I walked certain way-- you know, whatever. I got there, I was on the top-- how you call, not bed, but the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wooden-- bunks, yes. Matter of fact, I don't know if you want to use it. I took double mattresses with me. So I must have, you know, been driven with something. And this double-- I putted them doubled because I knew what kind of facilities there are.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the beginning, they left it this way, OK? And I took two suitcases. And this all-- because I wanted to separate like, and I slept with one of the girls together. And I stayed there maybe a month, not more. Tell me when you arrived what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived, I was shocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody came there with mattresses. And I thought they will kill me for it. So I was very scared. And then I was still sick. I couldn't eat anything that they had-- no soups, no, you know. I had to be on white bread and marmalade. That's how they treated this sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And no-- nobody got any medications. I mean, this was-- I had the doctor come when I was still in ghetto. And she said you can have macaron-- I mean, pasta, and white bread, and only sweets-- anything that has sugar in it, marmalade, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somehow, my aunts tried to get it for me. And that's how I survive. So there, I still probably once, I think, once, I wanted to have something hot. So I wanted this stupid soup. The whole night or two, I was throwing up. It was terrible. I thought that I am finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were so nice, the women around me. They, they would cover me. They would go to work, and they would cover me with all kinds of things that nobody would see that there is-- that I am there hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the beginning, OK? And then it was worse and worse. And I be-- had to work for a-- [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what this [NON-ENGLISH]? Building.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also so we had to go to the cemetery that was right next to it and pick up the stones, you know, from-- from the graves. And this was very, very hard work. We got those wheelbarrows, but it was terrible. And there were Ukrainers, from Ukraine in black-- in black clothes. They had uniforms-- black with silver.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very rude people. They beat everything. I was-- I don't know how nobody hit me, [LAUGHS] you know? It was impossible not to be hit. But, somehow, I had still long braids. They didn't shave us yet. So, maybe, who knows? I was lucky. Did they know you were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- yes. The kapo knew it, but she was a sister of my friend, one of the friends that we were singing together. And, to me, she was wonderful. Matter of fact, she did hide me in her own quarters. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: The women who were around you were older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And they were working with me. So they knew me. They knew my family. And so, you see, people from Cracow knew each other. And this helped in the beginning, OK? But then it started to be tighter and tighter. And we had to stand in appel. And whoever couldn't-- I was young. I could. But people that couldn't stand so long were all taken out and killed. And, yes, they would this way take-- from labor's camp, they made concentration camp because they were killing their people-- but not with oven, you know, not with-- in crematorium, but shooting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself? No. I heard about my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people you were living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there were no conditions, any kind of condition of hygienic, nothing. The water we used to have, I think, one, one pail like that, this was for the whole barrack. But the-- it was terrible. We felt like animals, like dogs. And when my uncle came and he said come with see me. I know somebody who will let you go to your mother's grave. And then he put me on a-- this cart that went to-- truck that went to ghetto. But then it starts what I told you before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle sent you back to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he said you cannot stay here. It's nothing for young girls. He was very much afraid of--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to leave Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he putted me with the group that went to ghetto to clean something. And this, he told me, don't talk to anybody. You just jump off the, the truck and mix yourself with, you know. Somehow-- you know, it's funny. When are young, you believe in everything. [LAUGHS] I believed that my mother and father are up there watching me. And God didn't let me save myself just for nothing. And this how we both lived through. And then, then we were in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: It has-- had to be about middle of '43, something like that. Because we did live with this people, and then I was told that my aunt-- two aunts went out from ghetto when it was Judenrat. Write it down, the word Judenrein. It was very important word. And there was a girl and a boy, so it was the aunt that I lived with her, with her son, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was-- he loved me so much, you know, that when I think-- he was 13 years old. No, 12 years old. And the girl was about his age. She looked more like my sister than my cousin's sister. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the-- was the arrangement like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had wonderful arrangements. They were in place where they had soldiers, German soldiers. And they couldn't go out as long as the soldiers were in the building. And they were in the cellar. And they could go out when they were for [POLISH], or whatever you called it, you know, exercises. And then, one day, somebody somehow probably told the wrong people, and they came and took them to Plaszow. Now, there was one man that had-- a Jewish man, that for one carat diamond, he can save a person. Not saved that goes out, but saved that will not be hanged.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had lots of jewellery. And one day, my aunt that was taking care of was wearing my mother's jewelry. And I started to cry. She says, how do you know it's your mother's? Hanka-- Aunt Hanka has the same engagement ring and this and that. And I told her no. My mother had in platinum and she had in gold. She says, how do you know? She didn't know it. But I remembered that my mother once compared with her sister. You are-- you have gold. Why did you put in gold or let him put in gold, not platinum? So this was under the table where I heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But so then I started to cry that she's taking that. And she says, look, we have to save four heads, two aunts. And she told me, I said, no, then you're right. And things like that were happening. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had false papers during this time? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Not this time. This time, we had no papers. But when we left, when the people threaten us that they will call Gestapo or whatever, so she got papers for us. I am sorry I didn't-- cannot show you right now, but I know I have it somewhere. And we got-- it was not really papers. It was not regular paper with photograph and so on. It was just a piece of paper like that from the unemployment office, German unemployment office. And it said my name, which was Janina Niezabitowska Abetovska, which was very, very good name. [LAUGHS] And it was aristocracy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin figured out just from her head, I want to be Irena Horodetska, OK? So two, two almost princesses left in Cracow. And we went, I remember, we went through Vienna with this piece of paper. How were you travelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. With railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Train?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, train. And-- regular train. Matter of fact, we had two women there, German, said she couldn't get over that we have both such a beautiful braids. Thank God, they didn't-- you know, that they left my hair. I mean, I didn't get to the point where they were cutting hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have occasion to use those papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We couldn't understand. As young we were, we couldn't understand that this was a paper that took us out, you know? Because it didn't look like anything. And then we got to Innsbruck. This is a place we could also just tell them what we-- where we want to go because we felt that the Innsbruck, Tirol will not be bombed. OK? That's how we believed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked in two hotels. My cousin was in one hotel. I was in the other one-- and cleaning the rooms, OK? And there was quite-- you know, I was not lucky there. I broke Hitler, Hitler's head. [LAUGHS] Because I was dusting it. I really didn't want to. And then I remembered-- Was this a bust of Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [LAUGHS] And then-- [LAUGHS] and then I was working in one room and I hit the hot water heating, you know, [POLISH]. I don't know. In Polish, it's [POLISH]. I don't how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a furnace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Furnace? Or something like that. And I also didn't know, all of a sudden, the hot water was out. You know, I broke this. I was lucky. They had so many reasons to lock me up, but that's where the things-- and then, later, we were-- we went to [GERMAN] which is the-- of course, German place where they could give you another job or something, but not for Polish you know, people, but for them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we went there and there were two young German students. And they were working at the desks. And, somehow, they couldn't believe it that we are here working because we looked like students. And, of course, they didn't know that we are Jewish, OK? [LAUGHS] So we stayed. Then she, my cousin, came to my hotel, and we were together there. It was terrible, even though they didn't know that we are Jewish because we had to play a role. And we had to go to church every Sunday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God I went to a Catholic school, not Catholic as a religion, but a Polish school. And I knew all those songs and whatever. And this is very important what I will tell you now. It was very important for us. We got a room at a very nice lady who did wear Hakenzreuz. She belonged to the party, but she was around 80 years old, and she liked us very much. And she let us live with her. We were very good to her. She was cooking for us, but we were good. When they heard bombs or something at night, some of those were our boys. So we were not afraid, so we waited for her, till slowly she would get dressed and we went to the cellar. In the cellar, we would talk to each other. Lots of people were crying. Women were crying, scared. I said look how lucky they are. Nothing can happen here. [LAUGHS] But, one day, we went to the church. And lots of people was there because they had three times you could go to-- go to church. Every hour, and all the prayers, whatever. And then we saw there are lots of-- how you call it-- the guys that-- OK. I will come to it. And there was a very, very big and very beautiful man in purple, and with purple hat, you know. This was from Vatican, somebody. And everybody was on the-- you know, they drop on their knees. And, of course, our woman, this lady couldn't, and we were standing next to her. And she says why don't you go on your knees. And we said, no, we cannot embarrass you. We had every answer for every question. It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we told her, no, we have to hold on to you. Where does he stop? In front of us. And whom does he bless? Me and my cousin. And we looked like [NON-ENGLISH] and we whispered, he knows, he knows. He knows we need him. So this was-- since then, we were Holy Marias. Every store-- it was a small place. Every store, wherever, like we went for milk, once a week, we could get glass of milk. So then, the owner of the business told us, girls, next time you bring me a two quart can because you spill it. We got two quart milk that was baby milk. So then-- [SIGHS] then when we went for cheese or something like that, because we had the card, only we had very limited amount. Of course, everybody had limited. We got just nothing. So they would give us a big piece of bread-- of butter. And you know, everything was for us, like I said, Holy Marias. So this was easy, except we were scared on every minute that somebody will recognize us. And then we worked for a factory. And it was very hard work because we didn't know how.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- they made fabrics for the soldiers, and everything was for the soldiers and so on. And that's how we live through. We behaved very nicely, and they respected us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- now, we're still in Innsbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are still in Innsbruck-- [NON-ENGLISH] where we lived. Innsbruck was also after I got married, after we were-- first, we had-- yeah. First, we had American soldiers that, you know, took over Innsbruck. And this was after May 5, after the war. Hitler gave up. And this how I met my husband there. He was Innsbruck. I was still in [NON-ENGLISH]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you met.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, tell me how you met.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we met. I tell you how we met. He was coming on a train-- not train, but a bus from Innsbruck to [NON-ENGLISH] because [NON-ENGLISH] had huge building with mineral waters and baths and things like that. And my cousin was sitting next to him. And he talked Hebrew, and she went to Hebrew gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it's was called gymnasium, but she didn't make more than just one grade, OK? And then they talk to each other in Hebrew, and she turned to him. She says [HEBREW]. And then they started to talk, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she took him to the place where we-- you know, we were still-- we were stupid. We were still working in the factory. We didn't want to take money from Jewish agency. We said money's for poor people. We are not poor. And then we also worked for-- actually, we created a office for people that wanted to go back to Poland. Of course, they didn't know that we are Jewish. But so she brought him. It was right next to the station.

speaker: interviewee
text: She brought him to us, to the office. And we took him to lunch. And it was-- look, you had only cards, all right? So we told him, and he would never forgive me. I said you can take two soups. [LAUGHS] It's not free, you know? So we had that. And then we, we did have some kind of a meal. And he started to come to visit us every other day when he went to, to go to the bath house. And little by little, that's how we-- What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, Abraham Teitelbaum. And we became very good friends. And he took us to the hotel that was left for us, for people from-- they were on the way to Switzerland. Lots of people wanted to go to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I got very sick. I had high temperature. And Mr. Abraham Teitelbaum was-- decided to take care of me. And if I didn't open my mouth to eat what he wanted me to eat, he threatened me he will kiss me. Can you imagine? [LAUGHS] So that's-- this was normal life, not like here. And-- Where were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in [? Wiesenhof, ?] was the name of the property. And then it turned to be hachshara, if you know what is hachshara. You know or shall I tell you? Hachshara was a place where they teach you how to live in a kibbutz. And we used to have lots of soldiers from Israel to help out the lessons and so on. And we had older person. He was a teacher, but he was in military gear. And he was very nice, and so on. And Abe was there, too. He was in-- he had a Mercedes, and he had a Jeep. And he was one of the guys that knew how to take care of the group of people that wanted to go to Israel, but not legally. You know that they stopped it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, at the time, you were still in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. And then, finally, we, we moved to Innsbruck where there was Jewish agency. And he worked with the Jewish agency. And then we decided to come or to-- legal-- to Israel or to America. And we putted our names in both. And I told him I don't want to go to another camp. I have to go legal. So whoever will be first, that's where we go. And United States got out papers first, so there. This how we managed to go to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in 8th of September, exactly. Which year?

speaker: interviewer
text: '46. And tell me what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was fun. We had a restaurant in Innsbruck that would cater the wedding. But my aunt-- the older aunt that was always around us-- I forgot to tell you. Are you tired? I'll stop talking. I think that you all want to know my life till normal times.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my aunt was in the kitchen. And, of course, we eat different things, not to talk about kosher at that time, because it was not. But on the day of our wedding, of course, the women were separate. The bride was separate from her groom. All of a sudden, somebody opens the door and brings a rabbi with his court. About four or five people walked in, very old man. And my husband was in heaven. He was quite orthodox, OK? And then he came to kiss me, normal, and to see if I am who I supposed to be. And I had lipstick on, and he gave me his handkerchief. And he says this doesn't go with me. So I whispered to him, don't worry, you are going to buy me lipsticks. [LAUGHS] So then we had a chuppah. And when I walk under-- you know, towards him, I was so young, but I felt like I thought this marriage will be 100% happy because I want it this way. And that how it was. And I really believe that a woman has lots to say about the wedding, you know, and the marriage. So then we-- he was, of course, working for the Jewish agency. And we moved to Innsbruck, as I say, because less and less people stayed in the place, the hachshara. So then after we got the papers, we had a good time in Innsbruck, OK? There is nothing to complain about it because, well, never mind. [LAUGHS] I cannot keep you so much and so long. But-- but then-- How long were you Innsbruck--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Innsbruck?

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding was-- I'll tell you how long. The wedding was in '46, and this was September. And we were there maybe two months before. And we arrived here in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: End of August.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So America was where you ended up coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewer
text: We should--

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: We should actually stop here and we'll travel.
